result:
I’ve been thinking about the way light lands on old brick walls at 5:17 p.m. in late April, when the sun has slipped low enough to become a private event, shared only by whoever happens to be walking past that particular wall at that particular minute. The bricks glow like they remember being clay, remember the hands that stacked them, the soot from coal trains, the graffiti that was painted and erased and painted again. For a moment they look semi-transparent, as if you could press your palm against them and feel every summer they’ve absorbed, every winter they’ve deflected, every argument and kiss and cigarette smoked in their shadow.

There’s a quiet alchemy in noticing something that isn’t trying to be noticed. No billboard, no push-notification, no algorithm predicted you would glance sideways at that wall. The moment isn’t hashtaggable; it refuses to scale. And yet it rearranges you a little, the way a stray sentence in a library book can make you close the covers and stare at the wall (a different wall, plaster not brick) and realize you’ve been holding your breath for years.

I keep a list of those untransferable moments, not in a notebook— that would feel like taxidermy— but in the muscle memory of how my stride shortens when I see pigeons wheel between buildings like scraps of gray silk caught in an invisible loom, or how the air smells when a subway grate exhales the ghost of last night’s rain. The list is useless to anyone else; it won’t get me a job, won’t raise my credit score, won’t even guarantee I’ll remember it tomorrow. Maybe that’s why I trust it.

We’re told to curate, to brand, to turn experience into content, but the bricks don’t curate themselves, and the pigeons don’t have a media kit. They just exist, the way the universe existed before any platform could ask “What’s on your mind?” Oddly, that thought feels like freedom: a permission slip to keep a piece of life offline, unoptimized, smelling faintly of iron and bread crusts.

So tonight I’m signing off early, leaving the screen’s blue glow to cool without me. I’m going to walk until I find a wall I’ve never seen, wait for 5:17 (or whatever time the sky decides), and let the light do its quiet, temporary surgery. No photo. No caption. Only the bricks and me, trading secrets neither of us will repeat.
